steps.m: to point to or draw attention
steps.n: This is a calling word used to get someone's attention
Root letters أ-ي-ي combined with fatha and shadda to make the question word 'which'
Added يٰٓ at beginning to make it a call for attention
Added damma at end to show this is in nominative case
Added ها to make it address a group of people
Added ن at the end to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال at the beginning and a shadda on the ذ to make it specific. This word points to the believers who are being addressed in this verse.
This is the basic meaning of trusting or feeling safe that all related words come from

steps.m: to make someone feel safe/to believe
steps.n: Adding alif at start changes meaning from 'feel safe' to 'make safe/believe'
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together
Added the small circle mark (۟) above وا to show it's a plural marker
This is a negative particle that prevents something from happening. Here it tells the believers not to take the enemies as allies.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense

steps.m: you (all) take/adopt (as a command after لا)
steps.n: The suffix وا shows plural 'you' and the word follows لا (do not) making it a negative command

Changes from verb 'being hostile' to noun meaning 'one who is very hostile/an enemy'
Added ى at the end to show possession (meaning 'my')

steps.m: to be hostile, to show enmity

steps.n: Changes the word into an adjective describing someone who has lasting enmity
Attached second person plural possessive pronoun to show ownership
Added و with fatha to connect this word to عَدُوِّى (my enemy) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: to be close to and protect

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from single protector/friend to multiple protectors/friends using special plural pattern أَفْعِلَاء

steps.m: close protecting friends/allies (as objects of لَا تَتَّخِذُوا - do not take)
steps.n: Added fatha at end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'do not take'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into past tense form

Added plural ending ون which causes the final ي to drop, and changes the middle vowel to match

Changed the final alif to yaa with sukun to prepare for adding the pronoun. This is a common change when adding pronouns to إِلَى.

steps.m: to them (referring to the enemies mentioned earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Added هِم to show we're talking about multiple people. In this ayah, it refers back to the enemies (عَدُوِّى وَعَدُوَّكُمْ) mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to feel or show love and affection

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added م at start and ة at end to turn the verb into an abstract noun meaning the concept of love
Added ال to specify we're talking about a particular instance of love
Added بِ at start to show this is done 'with' love, and ِ at end to connect it to the rest of the sentence
This is a particle that emphasizes that something definitely happened

steps.m: and indeed (connects to تُلْقُونَ - you offer)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect it to the previous statement about 'you offer'. This particle combination emphasizes that something definitely happened while connecting it to the previous thought.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The وا at the end shows that many people (they) did this action in the past
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in this ayah

steps.m: with what/with that which (connects to كَفَرُوا)
steps.n: Added بِ to show this is done 'with' or 'by means of' something. This connects to the verb كَفَرُوا to show what they disbelieved with/in.

Added the long alif (آ) to show emphasis in the coming action
Added كُم to show the action affects multiple people being spoken to
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or indicates a part of something
Added emphasis on the م because it comes after a word ending in a similar sound
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word الْحَقِّ (the truth). Together they mean 'from the truth'

Adding ال makes it specific ('the truth'), and the kasra at the end shows it follows the word مِنَ (from)

steps.m: to exit or make something/someone exit

steps.n: Basic past tense verb form meaning 'he exited' - vowels show it's a complete action
Added alif at start to make it mean 'made someone/something exit' instead of just 'exited'

Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from verb 'to send' to noun 'messenger' by adding و

steps.m: the messenger (as object of يُخْرِجُونَ - they expel)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is the object (receiver) of the action in يُخْرِجُونَ (they expel)
This is a special word used to emphasize pronouns

Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before it, like saying 'and'
This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence and introduces the verb تُؤْمِنُوا (you believe). It shows that believing is the thing being discussed.

steps.m: to cause to feel secure, to make safe
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
تُ at start shows this is about 'you' doing the action
The ون ending shows this is for a group of men

The كُمْ shows this refers to multiple people being addressed. The kasra (ِ) is added before كُمْ to connect it smoothly to the word.

Command form ('Be!') - the word is shortened to make it a command instead of 'he was'

Basic past tense form of the verb

Adds م prefix to make a noun showing where/when loving happens

Added ٱلْ to specify this particular affection
Added بِ at start to show 'with' and ِ at end because it follows بِ. This word shows how something is given in the phrase 'you offer to them with affection'
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun meaning 'I'
Added a small circle (۠) above the final alif to show this is the pronoun 'I', not just any word ending in alif
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb

steps.n: Added the preposition بِ (with/by) to show how something was done. In this ayah, it refers to what came to them from the truth that they rejected.
This is the basic meaning showing the action of hiding
Adding the vowels makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he hid'
Added hamza at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action. The final ى replaces ي because it's at the end of the word.
Added تُمْ at the end to show that 'you all' did the action in the past

Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, it's asking about what was hidden.

steps.m: to make something known or public
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning and a sukun (ْ) on the first root letter to make the action more direct and forceful
Added تُمْ at the end to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past
Combination of و (and) with من (who/whoever)

Added ال (the) at the start to make it 'the path' instead of just 'path'

steps.m: the path/way (being acted upon by فِى)

steps.m: towards you all/to you all (who are being addressed by the disbelievers)

Adds the final vowel to complete the plural form

an example, a model to follow

Added small ya (ۦ) as a pronunciation guide to show the kasra clearly
This is the basic past tense form of the verb
Adding the vowels makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'to feel safe'
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'someone who believes/trusts' instead of the action itself
Added ُونَ to show this refers to multiple believers. This word describes أَنتُم (you all) in this ayah.
The middle letter is doubled to make the meaning stronger
Added يا at the start to call attention, like saying 'hey!' in English. The alif is lengthened with a مد (stretch mark) to show the call is being extended
Added damma at the end to show this is in the nominative case, like adding 'the' in English
Added ها to make it a complete calling phrase, like saying 'hey you' instead of just 'hey'
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the basic noun form
Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show someone who does this action
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning, showing this is someone who regularly brings divine news
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda
Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject
This is the basic form of the word that indicates a point in time

steps.m: when, whenever (introduces condition for جَآءَكَ - comes to you)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it more general and conditional. It connects to the verb جَآءَكَ (comes to you) to show when something should happen.
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha marks) to make it mean 'he came' in the past
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Added كَ at the end to show the action was directed to 'you'. In this ayah, it's specifically addressing the Prophet ﷺ.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a past-tense verb (he felt safe) to a noun meaning someone who believes/trusts. The م at the start and the hamza in the middle are added to make it mean 'the one who does it'
Added feminine plural ending to show we're talking about multiple women who believe

steps.m: the believing women (who are coming to يُبَايِعْنَكَ - pledge allegiance to you)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show these women are doing the action in the verse (pledging allegiance)

steps.m: to exchange or make a deal

steps.n: Added alif to show this is something done between two parties - like shaking hands on a deal
Added يُ at start to show this is happening now or in the future
Added نَ at the end to show multiple women are doing the action (like adding 'they' instead of 'he')

steps.m: they (women) make a mutual pledge to you (addressing the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added كَ to show the pledge is being made to you (the Prophet ﷺ)

steps.m: on/upon (preposition connecting يُبَايِعْنَكَ with what follows)
steps.n: Basic form of the preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else

steps.m: on/upon (preposition connecting يُبَايِعْنَكَ with what follows)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word - 'fatha' on ع and ل

steps.m: on/upon (preposition connecting يُبَايِعْنَكَ with what follows)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ى with small alif on top) to show this is how the word ends when written in the Quran

steps.m: on/upon (preposition connecting يُبَايِعْنَكَ with what follows)
steps.n: Added a small vertical line above to show the sound is stretched slightly when reading

steps.m: that (connects to لَّا يُشْرِكْنَ - they do not associate)
steps.n: This is a special word called a particle that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it introduces what the believing women are pledging not to do. The fatha (َ) shows it's being used actively in the sentence.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُشْرِكْنَ (associate partners) that comes after it in the ayah. It's part of a pledge the believing women are making to not do certain things.

steps.m: absolutely not/definitely don't (negates يُشْرِكْنَ - associate partners)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) and fatha (َ) add emphasis to show this is a strong prohibition in the pledge. This emphasizes how serious it is to not associate partners with Allah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added نَ at the end to show this refers to multiple women doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the believing women (ٱلْمُؤْمِنٰتُ) making a pledge not to associate partners with Allah.
The نَ at the end shows multiple women are doing the action (feminine plural)
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda to emphasize this is THE one true deity, not just any deity
Added fatha on the shadda for proper pronunciation
Added بِ (bi) at the beginning to mean 'by/with', and the word gets a kasra (ِ) ending. The ٱ is just a special way of writing the alif when it comes after bi-.
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'a thing'

steps.m: anything (that is being associated with Allah)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, it's what the believing women promise not to associate with Allah.
Basic connecting word that joins this phrase to what came before
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added the word 'not' to make this a negative statement. In this ayah, it's part of a list of things that believing women promise not to do.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they (women) steal (part of a pledge to not steal)
steps.n: Changed to feminine plural by adding نَ and putting sukun on the last root letter. This word appears in a pledge where believing women promise not to steal.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يَسْرِقْنَ (they steal) in this ayah
Adding وَ connects this negative with the previous prohibition in the ayah

steps.m: he does this forbidden act (present tense)
steps.n: Added يَ at start to show it's happening now, and added vowels to connect the letters
Added نَ at the end to show many women are doing the action
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: and not (connected to يَسْرِقْنَ - they steal)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this negative statement with the previous one. This word is part of a series of things that believing women promise not to do.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed vowels and added يَ to show this happens in the present/future
Added نَ at the end to show multiple women are doing the action

steps.m: to give birth, to bear offspring

steps.n: Changed from action to the thing itself (from giving birth to the child)
Changes from one child to many children by breaking up the word pattern (broken plural)
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) but pronounced the same
Adds هُنَّ to show the children belong to multiple women
Starting with the basic particles و (and) and لا (not) written separately
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation. This word negates the verb يَأْتِينَ (they bring) in this ayah.
Added يَ at start to show 'he' in present tense, with dots under middle letter to make 'ee' sound
Added نَ at the end to show multiple women are doing the action
Changes from root meaning 'to falsely accuse' into a noun meaning 'false accusation' by adding vowel marks
Adds ان pattern to show intensity of the false accusation
Changes alif to alif maqsurah for writing style
Adds tanwin kasra to show indefiniteness

steps.m: with a serious false accusation (object of يَأْتِينَ)
steps.n: Adds preposition bi- to show how the action is done. This word is the object of يَأْتِينَ (they come) in this ayah.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added Form VIII pattern (اِفْتَعَلَ) to show doing the action to/for oneself
Changed to present tense by replacing initial اِ with يَ and adjusting vowels
Added نَ suffix to change from 'he' to 'they (women)' doing the action

steps.m: they (feminine) fabricate it (referring to بُهْتَان/slander in previous verse)
steps.n: Added هُۥ suffix as object pronoun meaning 'it', referring back to the slander/falsehood mentioned

steps.m: to be separate or between things

steps.n: Adding vowels changes this into a common preposition meaning 'between'
Connected the root letters to form the basic singular noun
Changed from singular 'hand' to plural 'hands' using broken plural pattern
Added the final vowel mark to complete the plural form
Added هِنَّ to show these are the hands belonging to a group of women
Added the vowel 'i' and sukoon marks to make it mean 'a foot/leg' instead of just the root concept
Added أ at start and changed vowels to make it plural - this is a broken plural pattern
Added 'and' at beginning and possession marker at end
Added هِنَّ to show these are the feet belonging to a group of women
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يَعْصِينَكَ (they disobey you) that comes after it in the ayah
Adds و at the beginning to connect this negative statement with the previous ones in the ayah, making it part of a list of things the believing women pledge not to do

steps.m: to disobey or refuse to follow orders

steps.n: Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added نَ to show multiple women are doing the action
Added كَ to show who is being disobeyed
This is a preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it shows that the women will obey in/regarding good matters.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مَ at the start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'something that is known' instead of 'to know'

steps.m: any good/acceptable deed (in matters of فِى)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra at the end to show this refers to any good deed in general, connecting to the word فِى (in) that comes before it in the ayah

steps.m: to make a pledge of loyalty

steps.n: Changes from verb (to pledge) to noun (one who pledges)
Adds فَ to show this command follows from what was mentioned before
Adds sukun to mark imperative form

steps.m: so make a mutual pledge with them (feminine plural)
steps.n: Adds هُنَّ to show the command is about making a pledge with multiple women, referring back to الْمُؤْمِنٰتُ (the believing women) mentioned earlier in the verse
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show seeking or requesting forgiveness
Changed from 'he sought' to 'you seek!' by changing the vowels to make it a command to one person

steps.m: and seek forgiveness! (command to Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect with previous command, and changed initial alif to alif wasl since it's now in middle of sentence

steps.m: for/to (connected to يَعْصِينَكَ - they disobey you)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing direction or purpose. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَعْصِينَكَ (they disobey you) to show what the believing women are being obedient about.

steps.m: for them (feminine plural, referring to the believing women)
steps.n: Adds هُنَّ which shows this refers to multiple women. This connects back to الْمُؤْمِنٰتُ (the believing women) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being described as غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - Most Forgiving, Most Merciful)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is being described. Also changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes in the middle of speech.

steps.m: indeed/truly (emphasizes that اللَّهَ is غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ)
steps.n: This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah is truly forgiving and merciful.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

steps.m: Allah (the One True God who is the object of يَسْتَغْفِرْ/seeking forgiveness)
steps.n: Changed from the general word for 'god' to the specific name 'Allah' by adding ال. The ending mark shows Allah is the one being asked for forgiveness.

steps.m: to forgive or cover up faults
one who forgives again and again (describing اللَّهَ)
steps.n: Added و and vowels to show this describes someone who forgives very much. This word describes Allah in this ayah.
The double dammah (ٌ) at the end shows this is Allah's name/quality of being Most Forgiving
Adding ي between the root letters makes it mean 'someone who is very merciful all the time'
Adding tanween (double damma) makes it indefinite, meaning 'an intensely merciful one'

steps.m: Most Merciful (describing Allah, paired with غَفُورٌ/Most Forgiving)
steps.n: The doubled ر (with shaddah) makes the word stronger, showing Allah's perfect and complete mercy. Often comes after غَفُورٌ (Most Forgiving) to show Allah's forgiveness brings His mercy
This is a particle used to call out to someone
Basic calling particle used to get someone's attention
The alif is written as a long vowel mark above the ي to show elongation when calling
أَيُّ is added to make the call more specific, like saying 'O you who...'
هَا makes the call address a group of people
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to a relative pronoun form
Added ين at the end to make it plural - now means 'those who' instead of 'one who'
Added ال with shadda to make it definite. This word describes the believers who are being addressed in this verse.
Added alif after first letter to make the meaning stronger and more specific to belief

steps.m: they believed (referring to the believers being addressed)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes the believers (ٱلَّذِينَ) mentioned in the ayah.

This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it (تَتَوَلَّوْا۟) into a negative command, like saying 'do not take as allies' in English. The fatha (َ) is added to show it's being used as a command.

steps.m: to be close to/take charge of

steps.n: Basic vowels added to make past tense verb meaning 'he became close to'
Added تَ at start and doubled ل to show doing action to oneself. The ى at end is due to weak letter rules.
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' as the subject
Added وا to show multiple people. This word connects to قَوْمًا (a people) in the ayah.

steps.m: people who stand together (acted upon by تَتَوَلَّوْا)
steps.n: Changed the action of standing together into a noun for the group that does it. This word is being acted upon by تَتَوَلَّوْا (do not take as allies) in this ayah.

steps.m: a people (who are being warned not to be taken as allies)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this word is receiving the action of the verb تَتَوَلَّوْا (do not take as allies)
Added vowels to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to يَئِسَ (despaired) in the ayah, showing parallel actions.
Changed to the specific name for the One God by adding ال and merging letters
Added shadda to emphasize the ل and added ُ to show this is the doer of the action 'became angry' in this ayah
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or against something else. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to ي to connect with the following pronoun. This is just a spelling change to help with pronunciation.

steps.m: upon them (referring to قَوْمًا - the people)
steps.n: Adds هِمْ at the end to mean 'them' (masculine plural). This refers back to قَوْمًا (the people) mentioned earlier in the verse.

This is a particle that adds emphasis to the verb يَئِسُوا۟ (they despaired) that comes after it. When قَدْ comes before a past tense verb like here, it means something definitely happened. When it comes before a present tense verb, it means something is likely to happen.
This root represents the concept of losing or giving up hope
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action
Basic preposition showing something comes from or originates from somewhere
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word ٱلْءَاخِرَةِ (the Hereafter). This is a pronunciation helper that makes the words flow better together.
This pattern is used to make a feminine noun from the active participle
Adding ة makes the word feminine
Added the definite article ال to make it specific. The آ changes to ءَا after ال according to Arabic spelling rules
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes before it in the ayah (they despaired of...)
Added fatha to the letter ك to make it readable

steps.m: just like, similar to (يَئِسَ - they despaired)
steps.n: Added ما to create a compound particle that makes comparisons. Here it compares how the disbelievers despaired of those in graves to how they despaired of the Hereafter.

steps.m: he lost hope (from ٱلْءَاخِرَةِ - the Hereafter)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to show this is about someone losing hope. In this ayah, it specifically refers to losing hope regarding the Hereafter.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled letter (shadda) and alif make this an intensive form, showing someone who strongly or habitually disbelieves
Changed from 'one disbeliever' to 'many disbelievers' by changing 'a' sound to 'u' sound at start
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular disbelievers

This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. Here it connects the verb يَئِسَ (they despaired) with أَصْحٰبِ (the companions/inhabitants), showing they despaired from/of the inhabitants.

steps.m: to accompany or be close to someone

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to accompany'
Changed from 'he accompanied' to 'companions' by using the plural pattern أَفْعَال (af'aal) - a common way to make plurals in Arabic
Changed regular alif to alif maqsurah - this is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to القُبُورِ (the graves) that comes after it
Changed from the root meaning 'to bury' into the plural word for 'graves' by adding the vowel marks ُ (damma) and و to show many
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to point to specific graves mentioned in the ayah - these are the graves of the disbelievers being referred to
Added ِ (kasra) at the end to show these graves belong to or are connected to the word أَصْحٰبِ (dwellers), forming the phrase 'dwellers of the graves'
